Few-shot relation extraction seeks to identify semantic relations between entities when only a handful of labeled examples are available. The rise of pretrained language models has reframed the problem from metric learning to prompt-conditioned inference with constrained supervision. Despite accuracy gains, current systems remain sensitive to prompt wording, label verbalizers, and domain shifts. This section reviews the landscape and clarifies the technical assumptions that shape the evaluation regimes in this area.

Early metric-based methods adapted prototypical representations and episodic training to approximate class structure under data scarcity. Subsequent transformer encoders introduced stronger contextualization but still depended on brittle hand-crafted templates. Task design choices around entity markers, sentence cropping, and negative sampling often dominate reported improvements. Moreover, inconsistent train–test splits and cross-domain generalization protocols complicate direct comparison across papers. These observations motivate a sharper look at the roles of prompts, representations, and data curation in few-shot settings.

Early prompt-based strategies for few-shot relation extraction emphasize pattern verbalization and label mapping with masked language models, including PET (Schick and Schütze, 2021), LM-BFF (Gao et al., 2021), and ADAPET (Tam et al., 2021). Subsequent approaches introduce continuous prompts and lightweight adapters for task conditioning, such as P-tuning (Liu et al., 2021), Prefix-Tuning (Li and Liang, 2021), and Prompt Tuning for T5 (Lester et al., 2021). Data resources commonly used to benchmark these methods include FewRel 1.0 and 2.0 (Han et al., 2018; Gao et al., 2019), TACRED (Zhang et al., 2017), and SemEval-2010 Task 8 (Hendrickx et al., 2010). Recent instruction-driven methods leverage larger language models via in-context learning and templated rationales, drawing on GPT-3 style setups (Brown et al., 2020), T0 (Sanh et al., 2021), Flan-T5 (Chung et al., 2022), and LAMA-style probing suites (Petroni et al., 2019) across standard evaluation protocols.

Together, these components reveal a field where modeling choices are intertwined with annotation granularity and label semantics. Prompt engineering implicitly encodes task definitions, blurring the line between representation learning and dataset specification. Robust evaluation should disentangle improvements due to scaling from those attributable to calibration or better verbalizers. Furthermore, label space entropy and relation argument structure can confound in-context learners if weak priors dominate. We therefore examine how template design, demonstration selection, and entity marking interact under controlled ablations.

Our analysis emphasizes reproducible splits, standardized verbalizers, and calibrated decoding under constrained resources. We revisit cross-domain transfer from newswire to Wikipedia, enforcing constant budgets for demonstrations and gradient steps. The study isolates prompt sensitivity from pretraining advantages by aligning tokenization and marker conventions across baselines. Finally, we outline a measurement model that attributes variance in accuracy to specific prompt and data factors.

Learning directly on graphs mitigates feature engineering and offers end-to-end adaptability to new endpoints. Yet performance often hinges on the alignment between molecular geometry, featurization choices, and the inductive biases of the encoder. We review techniques and evaluation protocols before presenting our study.

These considerations motivate principled data splits and careful normalization of input chemistry.

Detecting anomalies in multivariate telemetry is essential for safe and reliable operation of cyber-physical systems. Industrial sensors exhibit nonstationarity, missing data, and asynchronous sampling that complicate modeling. Supervised labels are scarce and often delayed, motivating semi-supervised and unsupervised formulations. We survey representative approaches and identify evaluation pitfalls that mask true generalization.

Classical baselines rely on robust statistics, autoregressive filters, and isolation-based schemes that provide interpretable alerts. Neural methods leverage representation learning to capture cross-channel dependencies without manual crafting. However, heavy smoothing and windowing decisions can suppress short-lived but critical transients. Threshold calibration is further complicated by nonstationary operating modes and maintenance interventions. These characteristics motivate principled scoring and uncertainty quantification for deployment.

Cross-institutional variability in scanners and protocols challenges models trained on single-site data. Augmentations can simulate plausible acquisition shifts but also risk violating anatomical realism. We review augmentation and pretraining practices in light of evaluation design.

Simple geometric transforms improve data coverage yet may distort organ topologies at high magnitudes. Intensity perturbations must respect modality-specific physics, particularly in MR sequences and CT Hounsfield units. Incorporating spatial priors through shape models can mitigate implausible deformations during training. Consistent preprocessing pipelines are necessary to isolate augmentation effects from resampling artifacts. These observations motivate principled ablations that separate regularization from genuine generalization.

Graph-structured data increasingly underpins search, recommendation, and knowledge management in large-scale systems. Effective node and edge representations are central to downstream accuracy and data efficiency. While supervision can be costly or domain-specific, self-supervision offers a scalable alternative through structure- and attribute-level signals. However, heterogeneous graphs pose additional challenges due to multiple relation types and schema complexity. The robustness of learned embeddings under distribution shift and sparse labels remains an open concern. This paper examines self-supervised objectives that leverage heterogeneity without relying on extensive manual curation.

Prior work has adapted contrastive formulations to graphs by maximizing agreement between correlated views. Many of these views are induced by stochastic augmentations, but their semantics can break under typed nodes and relations. We therefore consider how metapath constraints and relation-aware perturbations might preserve schema information during pretraining. Furthermore, batch negatives derived from unrelated subgraphs can be misleading when cross-type proximity governs informativeness. A unified lens that links augmentation semantics with heterogeneity is needed to clarify design trade-offs. We position our study at the intersection of augmentation design and scalable pretraining for multi-relational networks.

Despite steady progress, there is limited evidence on how contrastive signals should be routed across relation types in large benchmarks. We formalize this question by disentangling node, edge, and relation-level invariances in a single framework. Our analysis highlights when preserving metapath connectivity improves transfer versus when attribute masking is sufficient. We support these claims with controlled ablations and scale-sensitive evaluation protocols. The resulting perspective yields practical guidance for augmentation selection under heterogeneity and shift. We release code and reproducible pipelines to facilitate standardized comparisons.

Toxic language detection systems are increasingly deployed in content moderation and assistive writing tools. These systems must balance precision with harm minimization across sensitive demographic groups. Absent careful design, models can amplify stereotypes or produce disparate error rates. Variability in annotator perceptions and context length further complicates evaluation. Large pre-trained encoders offer strong baselines but inherit biases from training corpora. This section surveys datasets, metrics, and modeling practices that shape observed fairness outcomes.

A major challenge is separating offensiveness from identity mentions that are benign in context. Counterfactual and templated data augmentations attempt to control lexical confounding by swapping protected terms in matched sentences. While effective in isolation, these strategies can reduce coverage of dialectal variation and emergent slurs. Adversarial learning formulations target representation invariance but may conceal useful causal signals for moderation workflows. Practical deployments require calibration across thresholds to manage user-facing risk under traffic spikes. We thus consider evaluation regimes that combine distributional metrics with scenario-specific utility curves.

Despite diverse approaches, there is no consensus on the trade-offs between group fairness and contextual safety at scale. We study these tensions through stress tests that emulate platform shifts and adversarial paraphrases. Our analysis emphasizes the role of long-context modeling for disambiguating reclaimed terms and quoted speech. We introduce a protocol that disentangles thresholding from representation learning to isolate fairness effects. The findings point to practical configurations that maintain sensitivity without over-penalizing identity terms. We provide guidelines for auditing and documentation that align with policy and transparency requirements.

Traditional supervised pipelines optimize static objectives on logged interactions, yet deployment dynamics are sequential. Reinforcement learning offers a principled framework to model long-term value and exploration. However, online experimentation is expensive and risky, motivating offline or batch RL approaches. Logged feedback is selective and policy-dependent, which complicates learning and evaluation. This section outlines background on methods and datasets relevant to sequential recommendation.

Offline training introduces extrapolation error when policies act beyond the support of historical logs. Conservative objectives aim to suppress overestimation but can underfit diverse user intents. Ranking and slate constraints further challenge temporal credit assignment across multiple displayed items. We analyze how action-space parameterization affects both policy expressivity and off-policy evaluation variance. Our study also examines simulator fidelity and its interaction with counterfactual estimators. Together, these considerations motivate design choices that balance safety with learning efficiency.

We develop a modular pipeline that isolates data curation, policy learning, and evaluation. The pipeline supports heterogeneous slates, delayed rewards, and partial observability. A set of ablations quantifies the trade-off between conservatism and responsiveness under shifts. We report findings on synthetic and real datasets with stress-tested OPE diagnostics. These results inform practical deployment strategies for sequential recommenders under offline constraints.

Accuracy and robustness are critical because downstream measurements drive diagnosis and planning. Annotated medical data are scarce and heterogeneous due to privacy and acquisition differences. Transfer and self-supervised learning can mitigate these limitations through task-agnostic signals. Yet domain shifts across scanners, protocols, and institutions remain a primary obstacle. We review architectural and data-centric advances relevant to scalable medical segmentation.

Convolutional designs offer strong inductive biases for locality but can struggle with long-range context. Hybrid architectures incorporate attention to bridge cross-organ dependencies in volumetric inputs. Pretraining from natural images is common, though modality gaps complicate representation reuse. Reconstruction and masking objectives exploit abundant unlabeled scans to warm-start segmentation heads. Robust evaluation requires standardized preprocessing and clear separation between tuning and test cohorts. We focus on reproducible practices that facilitate cross-institutional transfer and fair comparison.
